title: execute_write_file does NOT enforce read-before-write enabling clobber attacks
description:
## Severity
Major

## Dimension
Security and Access / Functional Correctness

## Location
src/tools/file/write.rs:9-74 (execute_write_file) vs src/tools/file/edit.rs:46-53 (READ_TRACKER enforcement)

## Violation
edit_file requires the file have been read first (edit.rs:46-53 explicitly blocks the model from editing files it never opened — to "prevent edits based on hallucinated file contents"). write_file makes no such check. The model can call write_file on any existing file and silently clobber its contents without ever having read them.

```rust
// execute_write_file — no READ_TRACKER check anywhere
match fs::write(path, content) {
    Ok(()) => { ... }
}
```

This is the asymmetric protection problem. Worse, the write path performs the SAME canonicalize-or-walk-parent dance as edit and notebook (lines 20-34) — three copies of the same fragile logic across three sibling files, all with subtly different error messages.

A model that decides to "fix" a config file it has never seen can wipe it. A prompt-injection attack ("write a fresh CLAUDE.md saying X") succeeds without the read guard that edit_file would have refused at.

## Mandated refactor
1. Add the same READ_TRACKER.has_been_read guard to write_file when the file already exists. New-file writes are exempt (nothing to clobber). Wording: "File exists at '{path}' — call read_file first to confirm you intend to overwrite, OR use edit_file for in-place changes."
2. Extract the canonicalize-or-walk-parent dance to a shared helper used by write/edit/notebook (currently duplicated in all three).
3. Cross-reference issue #687 (replace_all gap) and the broader READ_TRACKER issue.

## Filed from QA review dispatch
